name: Symphony Sid
abstract: Sid Torin (born Sidney Tarnopol; December 14, 1909 – September 14, 1984), known professionally as "Symphony Sid", was a long-time jazz disc jockey in the United States. Many critics have credited him with introducing bebop to a mass audience.

concerts.label: Woody Herman and His Orchestra and The Nat King Cole Trio
Billy Eckstine, George Shearing Quintet, and The All Stars
Billy Eckstine, George Shearing Quintet, and The All Stars
Herbie Mann Sextet, Clara Ward Singers, and the Alegre All Stars

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Cobb
about_quotes.evidence: So our band had – our rhythm section had to play with them... They didn’t bring a rhythm section. So they used the rhythm section that was on the show. That was – that would be Dinah’s rhythm section, which at that time was Beryl Booker, Keter Betts, and myself. They went on three or four times a da
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Cobb — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Alvin Queen
about_quotes.evidence: Symphony Sid had Jump With My Boy Symphony Sid, that show, and I used to stay up late at night and I used to sneak the radio and put it in the room, and I’d plug the radio in and then that’s how I heard all the jazz and who was working at Birdland.
about_quotes.doc_title: Alvin Queen interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, August 23, 1997 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Frank "Machito" Grillo
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee mentions Symphony Sid as a partner in the Roost label and a 'good radio disk jockey' who 'know what he was talking about', but does not describe a personal interaction, collaboration, or direct professional relationship with him.

about_quotes.speaker: Cedar Walton
about_quotes.evidence: The guy, uh, Symphony Sid, the DJ, I guess you would call 'em now – we called 'em that then, a Disc Jockey. He would say, 'Yes, that was Mosaic composed by See-Dar Walton.'
about_quotes.doc_title: Cedar Walton — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Roy Haynes
about_quotes.evidence: people like Symphony Sid, who was a very popular DJ, they knew I was going to be called by the draft board, so they started teasing me, even on the radio.
about_quotes.doc_title: Roy Haynes — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Dan Morgenstern
about_quotes.evidence: Symphony Sid was important in this respect too, because Symphony Sid was the only d.j. at the time [who] played bebop on the radio. He was a proselytizer.
about_quotes.doc_title: Dan Morgenstern — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
